payload.aggregated_output:
# Anedot UI Monorepo

A monorepo for Anedot's design system and web applications.

## Project Structure

```
├── apps/
│   ├── org/          # Org application
│   ├── storybook/    # Storybook
└── packages/
    ├── config/       # Shared configuration
    ├── tokens/       # Design tokens
    └── ui/           # Core UI components
```

## Documentation

This project has several documentation resources to help you get started and work effectively

### Setup & Development

- [SETUP.md](./SETUP.md) - Complete local development environment setup guide
- [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md) - Contribution guidelines, coding standards, and design system rules

## Getting Started

### Prerequisites

- Node.js >= 18.2
- pnpm >= 10.20.0
- Git with GPG signing configured (see [GPG Signed Commits](./CONTRIBUTING.md#gpg-signed-commits))

### Quick Start

1. Clone the repository:

   ```bash
   git clone <repository-url>
   cd ui
   ```

2. Download, install, and run [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/compose/install/)

3. Run the automated setup script (recommended for macOS users):

   ```bash
   ./scripts/setup.sh
   ```

   The setup script automates the entire setup process including:

   - Installing dependencies (Homebrew, Node.js, pnpm, Nginx)
   - Setting up SSL certificates for local development
   - Configuring hosts and environment variables

   For full details on what the setup script does, see [SETUP.md](./SETUP.md).

4. Start development:

   ```bash
   pnpm dev
   ```

For detailed setup instructions, troubleshooting, and additional configuration options, please refer to [SETUP.md](./SETUP.md).

## Development

### Commands

- `pnpm dev` - Start development server
- `pnpm build` - Build all packages
- `pnpm lint` - Run ESLint
- `pnpm format` - Format code with Prettier
- `pnpm test` - Run tests
- `pnpm clean` - Clean build artifacts
- `pnpm storybook` - Start storybook
- `pnpm test:storybook` - Run storybook tests in CLI

### Design System

The design system is built on top of ShadcnUI with custom theming. It uses:

- Tailwind CSS for styling
- CSS Variables for theming
- Design tokens for consistency
- Radix UI for accessible primitives

### Adding New Components

1. Create component in `packages/ui/src/components`
2. Add stories in component directory
3. Add tests
4. Document props and usage
5. Update tokens if needed

## Recent Updates

### Sidebar Enhancements

- Added collapsible functionality with a toggle button positioned on the right edge
- Improved styling for the collapse button with hover effects
- Better organized sidebar content structure with header, content, and footer sections

### Account Switcher Improvements

- Added filtering capability for organization lists
- Implemented scrollable dropdown for many organizations
- Added keyboard shortcuts (⌘0, ⌘1-9, ⌘K)
- Improved visual styling with customizable role indicators

## Contributing

We welcome contributions to the Anedot UI monorepo! Please follow these guidelines:

### Contribution Workflow

1. **Setup your environment** - Follow the [setup instructions](#getting-started)
2. **Configure GPG signing** - All commits must be [GPG signed](./CONTRIBUTING.md#gpg-signed-commits)
3. **Create a branch** - Use a descriptive name following the pattern: `[username]/[feature-description]`
4. **Make your changes** - Follow our [coding standards](./CONTRIBUTING.md)
5. **Run tests and linting** - Ensure all tests pass with `pnpm test` and `pnpm lint`
6. **Submit a pull request** - Include a clear description of your changes
7. **Code review** - Address any feedback from reviewers

### Documentation

When contributing, please update relevant documentation:

- Component documentation in component directories
- Storybook stories for UI components
- README files for affected packages
- Main documentation files if necessary

### Design System Compliance

All contributions must follow our design system guidelines:

- Use consistent colors and spacing patterns
- Follow accessibility standards
- Maintain consistent component APIs

For comprehensive contribution guidelines, design system standards, and code conventions, see [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md).

## Quality Standards

- TypeScript strict mode enabled
- Zod for data validation and types from schemas
- Biome for lint and formatting (run `pnpm check` or `pnpm check:fix`)
- Husky for git hooks
- Storybook for documentation

### Testing

`pnpm test`: Use vitest with msw to mock endpoints. Keep these fast so we can run them
frequently. 

`pnpm e2e`: Playwright tests. Test "happy path" in each app. These are slow and
not exhaustive but ensure core user flows.

# Local Development Setup

This guide will help you set up your local development environment for the Anedot UI project.

## Initial Setup

First, be sure you have sudo access. 

### Misc Setup

Env vars

```bash
cp .env.template .env
```

### Installs Applications (MacOS)

Some of these are recommendations, use your best judgement

- [mise](https://mise.jdx.dev/) (or nvm) for node, pnpm, ruby, etc.
- pnpm
- node
- Docker
- Homebrew
- [Caddy](https://caddyserver.com/docs/)

run `pnpm install`

## Development

1. Make sure Docker is running
2. Make sure apps and packages are built

```bash
pnpm build
```

3. Start development:

```bash
pnpm dev
```

This command will:

- Run `./scripts/dev` starting all apps at once via caddyserver

## SSL Certificate

The setup script automatically:

1. Generates a self-signed SSL certificate
2. Adds it to your system keychain (on macOS)
3. Configures Nginx to use it

For non-macOS systems, you'll need to manually trust the certificate using your system's certificate manager.

## Environment Variables

The setup script creates a `.env` file with necessary configuration. If you need to modify any values:

1. Edit the `.env` file in the project root
2. Restart the development server

## Troubleshooting

If you encounter any issues:

1. `403` with blocked host errors

   Remove port number from [development.rb](<(https://github.com/anedot/anedot/blob/master/config/environments/development.rb#L10-L13)>) in `anedot/anedot`

   ```
   config.hosts << ".anedot.me:3000"
   ```

   to

   ```
   config.hosts << ".anedot.me"
   ```

2. Port conflicts:

   - The dev script automatically clears ports 3200-3299
   - You can manually check ports: `lsof -i :3200-3299`

3. SSL certificate issues:

   - Regenerate certificates: `rm -rf ./certs && ./scripts/setup.sh`
   - For macOS: Check System Keychain for "ui.anedot.me" certificate
   - For other systems: Manually trust the certificate in `./certs/local-cert.pem`

4. Hosts file issues:

   - Check /etc/hosts contains: `127.0.0.1 ui.anedot.me`
   - Try flushing DNS cache: `sudo dscacheutil -flushcache`

5. Command not found:

   - Make sure `~/.local/bin` is in your PATH
   - Try running `which uidev` to verify the command installation

6. Nginx issues:

   - Check Nginx status: `brew services list | grep nginx`
   - View Nginx logs: `tail -f /opt/homebrew/var/log/nginx/error.log`
   - Restart Nginx: `sudo brew services restart nginx`

7. Node.js/NVM issues:
   - Verify NVM installation: `command -v nvm`
   - Check Node version: `node --version`
   - Try reinstalling Node: `nvm install --lts && nvm use --lts`

- Tailwind CSS for styling
- CSS Variables for theming
- Design tokens for consistency
- Radix UI for accessible primitives

### Adding New Components

1. Create component in `packages/ui/src/components`
2. Add stories in component directory
3. Add tests
4. Document props and usage
5. Update tokens if needed

## Recent Updates

### Sidebar Enhancements

- Added collapsible functionality with a toggle button positioned on the right edge
- Improved styling for the collapse button with hover effects
- Better organized sidebar content structure with header, content, and footer sections

### Account Switcher Improvements

- Added filtering capability for organization lists
- Implemented scrollable dropdown for many organizations
- Added keyboard shortcuts (⌘0, ⌘1-9, ⌘K)
- Improved visual styling with customizable role indicators

## Contributing

We welcome contributions to the Anedot UI monorepo! Please follow these guidelines:

### Contribution Workflow

1. **Setup your environment** - Follow the [setup instructions](#getting-started)
2. **Configure GPG signing** - All commits must be [GPG signed](./CONTRIBUTING.md#gpg-signed-commits)
3. **Create a branch** - Use a descriptive name following the pattern: `[username]/[feature-description]`
4. **Make your changes** - Follow our [coding standards](./CONTRIBUTING.md)
5. **Run tests and linting** - Ensure all tests pass with `pnpm test` and `pnpm lint`
6. **Submit a pull request** - Include a clear description of your changes
7. **Code review** - Address any feedback from reviewers

### Documentation

When contributing, please update relevant documentation:

- Component documentation in component directories
- Storybook stories for UI components
- README files for affected packages
- Main documentation files if necessary

### Design System Compliance

All contributions must follow our design system guidelines:

- Use consistent colors and spacing patterns
- Follow accessibility standards
- Maintain consistent component APIs

For comprehensive contribution guidelines, design system standards, and code conventions, see [CONTRIBUTING.md](./CONTRIBUTING.md).

## Quality Standards

- TypeScript strict mode enabled
- Zod for data validation and types from schemas
- Biome for lint and formatting (run `pnpm check` or `pnpm check:fix`)
- Husky for git hooks
- Storybook for documentation

### Testing

`pnpm test`: Use vitest with msw to mock endpoints. Keep these fast so we can run them
frequently. 

`pnpm e2e`: Playwright tests. Test "happy path" in each app. These are slow and
not exhaustive but ensure core user flows.

# Org Next

## Conventions

Follow TanStack standards and recommendations as much as possible.

## File Naming

- Files with *.functions.ts are `createServerFn` functions that can be called on
  server or client
- Files with *.server.ts are `createServerOnlyFn` functions that can only be
  called on the server

## Directories

- `src/lib` is for pure functions like utilities but please be specific, no file
  should ever be named `utils`
- `src/data` is for api calls

## End-to-end tests

Playwright starts its own dev server on **port 3210** (`pnpm e2e`). Do not run a
second `vite dev` on that port at the same time, or the run will fail to bind.

# AnedotAuth — Technical Spec

## Overview

AnedotAuth is a standalone application that replaces the AWS Cognito Hosted UI with a custom-built login experience. It serves as the centralized authentication layer for all Anedot client applications (3-4 initially), providing a consistent login experience via standard OAuth2/OIDC redirect flows.

**Key decisions:**
- TanStack Start app deployed to Cloudflare Workers
- Authorization code flow
- Google federated login via Cognito (redirect-based, not client-side Google SDK)
- SSO delegated entirely to Cognito — no custom session management
- Credential and challenge API calls happen server-side in AnedotAuth; client apps still exchange their own authorization codes for Cognito tokens

---

## Architecture

### How It Works

AnedotAuth sits between the client apps and Cognito, replacing the hosted UI in the redirect chain. The flow from a client app's perspective is unchanged — they redirect to an authorize URL and receive a code back.

```
Client App → AnedotAuth → Cognito (or Google via Cognito) → Client App
```

AnedotAuth is a thin UI layer. It renders login screens, handles form submissions by calling Cognito server-side, and redirects back to the client app on success. It does not manage sessions — Cognito is the sole session authority.

### How Client Apps Authenticate Requests

AnedotAuth handles login and issues tokens — but the client apps are responsible for using those tokens to authenticate their own API requests. The flow works like this:

1. After the OAuth redirect, the client app exchanges the authorization code for tokens (ID token, access token, refresh token) via Cognito's token endpoint using the PKCE code verifier.
2. The client app stores these tokens in memory (or however it manages auth state).
3. For each API request, the client app attaches the access token in the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header.
4. The backend API validates the token by checking the signature against Cognito's JWKS (JSON Web Key Set), verifying the expiry, audience, and issuer claims. Most frameworks have middleware for this.
5. When the access token expires (typically after 1 hour), the client app uses its refresh token to get a new access token from Cognito's token endpoint. This happens silently — no redirect to AnedotAuth needed.
6. If the refresh token is also expired or revoked (e.g. via `AdminUserGlobalSignOut`), the silent refresh fails. The client app should then redirect back to AnedotAuth, which will attempt an SSO check via Cognito. If the Cognito session is also expired, the user sees the login screen.

AnedotAuth is only involved at the start of the flow and when the client app's tokens are fully expired and can't be refreshed. Cognito still issues the final authorization code directly to the client app, and all request-level authentication is between the client app and its backend using standard JWT validation.

### Auth Flow (Username/Password)

1. Client app redirects user to `https://auth.anedot.com/authorize?client_id=X&redirect_uri=Y&response_type=code&scope=openid+email+profile&state=Z`
2. AnedotAuth server-side validates the request and attempts a Cognito-backed silent SSO check
3. If Cognito has an active session → Cognito redirects directly to the client app's `redirect_uri` with an authorization code and state. User never sees a login screen.
4. If no active session → AnedotAuth renders the sign-in screen
5. User enters credentials, submits the form
6. AnedotAuth server-side calls Cognito's authentication APIs for the custom login flow
7. On success → the browser is redirected into Cognito's `/authorize` flow so Cognito can issue the final authorization code directly to the client app's `redirect_uri`
8. Client app exchanges the code for tokens with Cognito's token endpoint

### Auth Flow (Google)

1. Client app redirects to AnedotAuth, optionally with `identity_provider=Google`
2. AnedotAuth redirects to Cognito's `/authorize` endpoint with `identity_provider=Google`
3. Cognito redirects to Google
4. Google authenticates and redirects back to Cognito
5. Cognito establishes a session and redirects directly to the client app's `redirect_uri` with an authorization code
7. Client app exchanges the code for tokens

### SSO Flow

SSO is handled entirely by Cognito's session cookies on the Cognito domain. When a user has already logged in via one client app:

1. Second client app redirects user to AnedotAuth
2. AnedotAuth redirects to Cognito's `/authorize` endpoint
3. Cognito detects an active session (via its own cookies), skips the login prompt, and redirects directly to the client app with an authorization code
4. User sees a brief redirect flash but no login screen

Refresh token lifetime is controlled by the Cognito user pool configuration, while Cognito's hosted browser session is a separate concern. `AdminUserGlobalSignOut` revokes the user's tokens, but browsers still need to hit Cognito's `/logout` endpoint to clear Cognito-hosted session cookies.

### Logout

1. Client app redirects to `https://auth.anedot.com/logout?client_id=X&logout_uri=Y`
2. AnedotAuth validates `client_id` and `logout_uri`
3. AnedotAuth server-side redirects the browser to Cognito's `/logout` endpoint
4. Cognito clears its hosted session cookies and redirects to the validated `logout_uri`

---

## Deployment

TanStack Start app deployed to **Cloudflare Workers** via TanStack Start's Cloudflare adapter, hosted at `auth.anedot.com`.

This is a single deployment artifact — the TanStack Start app handles both server-side route handlers (Cognito API calls, redirects) and client-side rendering (login screens, form interactions). No separate Worker, no KV namespace, no session storage infrastructure.

**DNS:** `auth.anedot.com` is managed through Cloudflare. The rest of your DNS can remain on Route 53.

### Redirect URI Validation

Allowed redirect URIs are configured per `client_id` as an environment variable (managed via `wrangler secret` or CI/CD). AnedotAuth validates `redirect_uri` and `logout_uri` against this whitelist on every request. Reject and show an error for any unrecognized redirect URI — never redirect to an unvalidated URL.

---

## URL Contract

### Entry Points

AnedotAuth exposes two URLs that client apps interact with:

**`/authorize`**

| Parameter | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `client_id` | Yes | Cognito app client ID |
| `redirect_uri` | Yes | Where to send the user after auth |
| `response_type` | Yes | Always `code` |
| `scope` | Yes | e.g. `openid email profile` |
| `state` | Yes | Opaque string, passed back untouched (CSRF protection) |
| `identity_provider` | No | Set to `Google` to skip the login screen and go straight to Google |

**`/logout`**

| Parameter | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `logout_uri` | Yes | Where to redirect after logout |
| `client_id` | Yes | Required for validation and Cognito logout |

---

## Screens

### 1. Sign In

The default screen when a user arrives (only shown if the SSO check against Cognito found no active session).

**Elements:**
- Email input
- Password input
- "Sign in" button
- "Sign in with Google" button (initiates the Google federation flow)
- "Forgot password?" link → navigates to Forgot Password screen
- "Create account" link → navigates to Sign Up screen

**Behavior:**
- On submit, server-side calls Cognito `initiateAuth` with `USER_SRP_AUTH`
- On success → redirect to client app
- On `NEW_PASSWORD_REQUIRED` challenge → navigate to Force Change Password screen
- On `SMS_MFA` or `SOFTWARE_TOKEN_MFA` challenge → navigate to MFA Challenge screen
- On `UserNotConfirmedException` → navigate to Confirm Sign Up screen, pre-filling the email
- On error → display inline error message

**Cognito errors to handle:**
- `NotAuthorizedException` → "Incorrect email or password"
- `UserNotFoundException` → same message as above (don't reveal whether account exists)
- `UserNotConfirmedException` → redirect to confirmation
- `TooManyRequestsException` → "Too many attempts. Please try again later."

### 2. Sign Up

**Elements:**
- Email input
- Password input
- Confirm password input
- "Create account" button
- "Already have an account? Sign in" link

**Behavior:**
- On submit, server-side calls Cognito `signUp`
- On success → navigate to Confirm Sign Up screen
- Validate password client-side against Cognito password policy before submitting

**Cognito errors to handle:**
- `UsernameExistsException` → "An account with this email already exists"
- `InvalidPasswordException` → display the password requirements
- `TooManyRequestsException` → rate limit message

### 3. Confirm Sign Up

**Elements:**
- Informational text ("We sent a verification code to {email}")
- Verification code input
- "Confirm" button
- "Resend code" link

**Behavior:**
- On submit, server-side calls Cognito `confirmSignUp`
- On success → automatically call `initiateAuth` server-side with the user's email and password (held in server state from the sign-up step), then redirect back to the client app. The user should not see the sign-in screen.
- "Resend code" calls `resendConfirmationCode`

# Anedot Auth App

Standalone auth portal for Anedot client applications.

## Local development

Local development defaults to mock auth mode (`VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=true`) so the UI and route logic can be developed without a live Cognito environment.

Run:

`pnpm --filter auth dev`

Open:

`https://auth.anedot.me:8443/authorize?...`

## Integration mode

Set `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=false` and provide real Cognito env values plus
`AUTH_ALLOWED_URIS_BY_CLIENT_ID` (JSON map of `client_id` -> allowed redirect/logout URIs)
to exercise real redirect orchestration and higher-environment auth validation.

## Environment Variables

### Required

- `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE`
  - `true` enables local/mock auth flows.
  - `false` enables Cognito-backed auth flows.

- `AUTH_ALLOWED_URIS_BY_CLIENT_ID`
  - JSON object mapping each `client_id` to its allowed redirect/logout URIs.
  - Used to validate both `redirect_uri` and `logout_uri` before any redirect.
  - Example:
    - `{"local-client":["https://org.anedot.me/oauth","https://org.anedot.me/logout"]}`

- `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`
  - Secret used to sign/encrypt auth session data.
  - Use a strong value (at least 32 characters) in non-local environments.
  - Startup/deploy fails fast when this variable is missing, insecure, or too short.

### Required when `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=false`

- `COGNITO_DOMAIN` - Cognito hosted UI domain URL.
- `COGNITO_CLIENT_ID` - Cognito app client ID used by AnedotAuth.
- `COGNITO_CLIENT_SECRET` - Cognito app client secret (if configured).
- `COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID` - Cognito user pool ID.
- `COGNITO_REGION` - AWS region for the user pool (for example `us-west-2`).

## End-to-end tests

Playwright starts its own preview server on **port 3208** locally (**3218** in
CI) (`pnpm e2e`). Do not run another process on that port at the same time, or
the run will fail to bind.

Playwright starts its own dev server on **port 3210** (`pnpm e2e`). Do not run a
second `vite dev` on that port at the same time, or the run will fail to bind.

apps/auth/docs/spec.md:20:AnedotAuth sits between the client apps and Cognito, replacing the hosted UI in the redirect chain. The flow from a client app's perspective is unchanged — they redirect to an authorize URL and receive a code back.
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:26:AnedotAuth is a thin UI layer. It renders login screens, handles form submissions by calling Cognito server-side, and redirects back to the client app on success. It does not manage sessions — Cognito is the sole session authority.
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:30:AnedotAuth handles login and issues tokens — but the client apps are responsible for using those tokens to authenticate their own API requests. The flow works like this:
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:32:1. After the OAuth redirect, the client app exchanges the authorization code for tokens (ID token, access token, refresh token) via Cognito's token endpoint using the PKCE code verifier.
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:33:2. The client app stores these tokens in memory (or however it manages auth state).
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:34:3. For each API request, the client app attaches the access token in the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header.
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:35:4. The backend API validates the token by checking the signature against Cognito's JWKS (JSON Web Key Set), verifying the expiry, audience, and issuer claims. Most frameworks have middleware for this.
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:36:5. When the access token expires (typically after 1 hour), the client app uses its refresh token to get a new access token from Cognito's token endpoint. This happens silently — no redirect to AnedotAuth needed.
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:37:6. If the refresh token is also expired or revoked (e.g. via `AdminUserGlobalSignOut`), the silent refresh fails. The client app should then redirect back to AnedotAuth, which will attempt an SSO check via Cognito. If the Cognito session is also expired, the user sees the login screen.
apps/auth/docs/spec.md:39:AnedotAuth is only involved at the start of the flow and when the client app's tokens are fully expired and can't be refreshed. Cognito still issues the final authorization code directly to the client app, and all request-level authentication is between the client app and its backend using standard JWT validation.

# frozen_string_literal: true

class CognitoJwtValidator
  class InvalidToken < StandardError; end

  JWKS_CACHE_KEY = "cognito_jwks"
  JWKS_CACHE_TTL = 1.hour

  def initialize(token)
    @token = token
    @config = Rails.application.config.cognito
  end

  def decode
    raise InvalidToken, "Missing token" if @token.blank?

    user_pool_id = @config[:user_pool_id]
    client_id = @config[:client_id]
    region = @config[:region]

    raise InvalidToken, "Cognito not configured" if user_pool_id.blank? || client_id.blank?

    iss = "https://cognito-idp.#{region}.amazonaws.com/#{user_pool_id}"

    payload, _header = JWT.decode(
      @token,
      nil,
      true,
      algorithms: [ "RS256" ],
      jwks: jwks_loader,
      iss: iss,
      verify_iss: true,
      aud: client_id,
      verify_aud: false
    )

    payload
  rescue JWT::DecodeError, JWT::JWKError => e
    raise InvalidToken, e.message
  end

  private

  def jwks_loader
    ->(_options) do
      jwks_hash = fetch_jwks
      jwks = JWT::JWK::Set.new(jwks_hash)
      jwks.filter! { |key| key[:use] == "sig" }
      jwks
    end
  end

  def fetch_jwks
    Rails.cache.fetch(JWKS_CACHE_KEY, expires_in: JWKS_CACHE_TTL) do
      uri = jwks_uri
      response = Net::HTTP.get_response(uri)
      raise InvalidToken, "Failed to fetch JWKS" unless response.is_a?(Net::HTTPSuccess)

      JSON.parse(response.body).symbolize_keys
    end
  end

  def jwks_uri
    region = @config[:region]
    user_pool_id = @config[:user_pool_id]
    URI("https://cognito-idp.#{region}.amazonaws.com/#{user_pool_id}/.well-known/jwks.json")
  end
end

# frozen_string_literal: true

module CognitoJwtAuthentication
  extend ActiveSupport::Concern

  included do
    before_action :authenticate_request!
  end

  private

  def authenticate_request!
    @current_user = User.last || User.create!(email: "admin@anedot.com", cognito_sub: "admin")
    # token = bearer_token
    # payload = CognitoJwtValidator.new(token).decode
    # sub = payload["sub"]
    # @current_user = User.find_by(cognito_sub: sub) || create_user_from_cognito(payload)
    # rescue CognitoJwtValidator::InvalidToken
    # render json: { error: "Unauthorized" }, status: :unauthorized
  end

  def bearer_token
    auth = request.authorization
    return nil if auth.blank?

    match = auth.match(/\ABearer\s+(.+)\z/)
    match ? match[1].strip : nil
  end

  def current_user
    @current_user
  end

  def create_user_from_cognito(payload)
    sub = payload["sub"]
    email = payload["email"]
    first_name = payload["given_name"]
    last_name = payload["family_name"]

    User.create!(cognito_sub: sub, email: email).tap do |user|
      Invite.invited.where(email: email).update_all(user_id: user.id)
    end
  end
end

# frozen_string_literal: true

# AWS Cognito JWT authentication. Set in ENV:
#   COGNITO_REGION       - AWS region (default: us-east-1)
#   COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID - User pool ID
#   COGNITO_CLIENT_ID    - App client ID (used as JWT audience)
Rails.application.config.cognito = {
  region: ENV.fetch("COGNITO_REGION", "us-east-1"),
  user_pool_id: ENV.fetch("COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID", nil),
  client_id: ENV.fetch("COGNITO_CLIENT_ID", nil)
}.freeze

# frozen_string_literal: true

class UsersController < ApplicationController
  before_action :set_user, only: %i[show update]

  def me
    render json: current_user
  end

  def index
    render json: User.all
  end

  def show
    render json: @user
  end

  def create
    user = User.new(user_params)
    if user.save
      render json: user, status: :created
    else
      render json: { errors: user.errors.full_messages }, status: :unprocessable_entity
    end
  end

  def update
    if @user.update(user_params)
      render json: @user
    else
      render json: { errors: @user.errors.full_messages }, status: :unprocessable_entity
    end
  end

  private

  def set_user
    @user = User.find(params[:id])
  end

  def user_params
    params.require(:user).permit(:email, :cognito_sub)
  end
end

### Scoping is a query parameter, not a URL segment

```
GET /campaigns?tenant_id=1             → org user's campaigns
GET /campaigns?tenant_id=1&vendor_id=2 → vendor's view of client campaigns
```

The `vendor_id` parameter is the signal that the request is in "vendor mode." The controller layer handles the scoping logic — callers don't need to know about `VendorTenantLink` or `VendorAccessGrant`.

### Authentication is external

The app doesn't manage passwords, sessions, or tokens. AWS Cognito owns the identity layer. The API's only responsibility is:

1. Extract the Bearer token from the `Authorization` header
2. Validate the JWT signature against Cognito's public keys
3. Look up (or create) the `User` by Cognito `sub`

This is intentional: identity is a solved problem, and delegating it to Cognito means the app never stores credentials.

### Read-only vs. mutable resources

Some resources are intentionally read-only via the API:

- **Payments** — Created by the payment processing pipeline, not by API users
- **Merchants/Submerchants** — Managed through the merchant application process and external systems (SubFi)
- **Campaign Contacts** — Created as a side effect of contact creation, not directly

This reflects a philosophy that **the API exposes what the user should control** and hides internal plumbing.

### JSON everywhere

Request and response bodies are JSON. The `settings` and `metadata` fields use `jsonb` columns, giving structured extensibility without schema migrations for every new configuration option. Campaign page settings can store amounts, form fields, redirect URLs, and embed domains — all without changing the database schema.

---

## System Boundaries

### What the Rails app owns

- Data storage and business rules (validations, state machines, scoping)
- Authentication verification (JWT validation)
- API surface (RESTful JSON endpoints)
- OpenAPI spec generation (introspection-based, auto-generated from code)

### What external systems own

- **AWS Cognito** — User identity, authentication, token issuance
- **SubFi** — Payment processing, merchant onboarding, charge/refund execution
- **The frontend** — Presentation, form management, client-side routing

### How the frontend fits

# frozen_string_literal: true

class ApplicationController < ActionController::API
  include CognitoJwtAuthentication
  include VendorAuthorization

  rescue_from ActiveRecord::RecordNotFound, with: :record_not_found

  before_action :set_vendor

  def prevent_vendor_access
    raise ActionController::RoutingError, "Not Found" if @vendor.present?
  end

  private

  def set_vendor
    @vendor = current_user.tenants.vendors.find(params[:vendor_id]) if params[:vendor_id].present?
  end

  def set_tenant
    tenant_id = params[:tenant_id] || params[:id]

    @tenant = if @vendor.present?
      link = VendorTenantLink.active.find_by!(
        vendor_tenant_id: @vendor.id,
        client_tenant_id: tenant_id
      )
      Tenant.find(link.client_tenant_id)
    else
      current_user.tenants.find(tenant_id)
    end
  end

  def campaign_scope
    set_tenant
    scope = @tenant.campaigns
    return scope if @vendor.blank?

    scope.where(vendor_id: @vendor.id)
  end

  def record_not_found
    render json: { error: "Record not found" }, status: :not_found
  end
end

# frozen_string_literal: true

class User < ApplicationRecord
  has_many :tenant_memberships, dependent: :destroy
  has_many :tenants, through: :tenant_memberships
  has_many :invites, dependent: :destroy
  has_many :sent_invites, class_name: "Invite", foreign_key: :invited_by_id, dependent: :destroy, inverse_of: :invited_by

  validates :email, presence: true, uniqueness: true
  validates :cognito_sub, uniqueness: true, allow_nil: true
end

# frozen_string_literal: true

# Seeds are idempotent - safe to run multiple times.
# Covers every table with realistic, varied data.

# =============================================================================
# Users
# =============================================================================
puts "Seeding users..."

admin     = User.find_or_create_by!(email: "admin@example.com")
alice     = User.find_or_create_by!(email: "alice@example.com")
bob       = User.find_or_create_by!(email: "bob@example.com")
vendor_u  = User.find_or_create_by!(email: "vendor@example.com")
unlinked  = User.find_or_create_by!(email: "unlinked@example.com")

# =============================================================================
# Tenants
# =============================================================================
puts "Seeding tenants..."

org_acme = Tenant.find_or_create_by!(name: "Acme Campaign") do |t|
  t.tenant_type = "org"
end

org_hope = Tenant.find_or_create_by!(name: "Hope Foundation") do |t|
  t.tenant_type = "org"
end

vendor_co = Tenant.find_or_create_by!(name: "Vendor Co") do |t|
  t.tenant_type = "vendor"
end

TenantMembership.find_or_create_by!(user: admin, tenant: org_hope)
TenantMembership.find_or_create_by!(user: admin, tenant: vendor_co)

TenantMembership.find_or_create_by!(user: alice, tenant: org_acme)

TenantMembership.find_or_create_by!(user: vendor_u, tenant: vendor_co)

# =============================================================================
# Vendor Tenant Links
# =============================================================================
puts "Seeding vendor tenant links..."

vtl_active = VendorTenantLink.find_or_create_by!(
  vendor_tenant: vendor_co,
  client_tenant: org_acme
) { |v| v.status = "active" }

vtl_revoked = VendorTenantLink.find_or_create_by!(
  vendor_tenant: vendor_co,
  client_tenant: org_hope
) do |v|
  v.status = "revoked"
  v.revoked_at = 2.weeks.ago
end

# Vendor Access Grants
# =============================================================================
# NOTE: The active VendorTenantLink (vtl_active) auto-provisions default grants

  vendor_tenant: vendor_co, client_tenant: org_acme, scope: "donors"
)
grant_donors.update!(permissions_json: { "can_view_donors" => true, "restricted_fields" => %w[phone email] })

grant_exports = VendorAccessGrant.find_by!(
  vendor_tenant: vendor_co, client_tenant: org_acme, scope: "exports"
)
grant_exports.update!(permissions_json: { "can_export_payments" => true })

VendorAccessGrant.find_or_create_by!(
  vendor_tenant: vendor_co,
  client_tenant: org_acme,
  scope: "everything"
) { |g| g.role = "admin" }

# =============================================================================
# Merchants
# =============================================================================
puts "Seeding merchants..."

merchant_acme = Merchant.find_or_create_by!(tenant: org_acme, legal_name: "Acme LLC") do |m|

end

merchant_hope = Merchant.find_or_create_by!(tenant: org_hope, legal_name: "Hope Foundation Inc") do |m|
  m.dba_name = "Hope Giving"
  m.state = "active"
  m.subfi_merchant_id = "subfi_merch_hope"
end

# =============================================================================

# =============================================================================

sub_acme_donations = Submerchant.find_or_create_by!(merchant: merchant_acme, mcc: "8661") do |s|
  s.subfi_account_id = "subfi_acc_acme_8661"
  s.name = "Acme Donations"
end

sub_acme_events = Submerchant.find_or_create_by!(merchant: merchant_acme, mcc: "7941") do |s|
  s.subfi_account_id = "subfi_acc_acme_7941"
  s.name = "Acme Events"

sub_hope_donations = Submerchant.find_or_create_by!(merchant: merchant_hope, mcc: "8661") do |s|
  s.subfi_account_id = "subfi_acc_hope_8661"
  s.name = "Hope Donations"

# Merchant Applications

puts "Seeding merchant applications..."

# Anedot Next

## Documentation

- [Architecture & Philosophy](docs/architecture.md) — Core concepts, data model, API design principles, and key decisions

- [Cognito Testing](docs/cognito-testing.md)

---

# Obtaining JWT via Cognito

Login at:

```
https://login-qa.anedot.com/login?client_id=CLIENT_ID&response_type=token&scope=email+openid&redirect_uri=http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%3A3000%2Foauth%2Fcallback
```

On success you'll be redirected to:

```
http://localhost:3000/oauth/callback#id_token=ID_TOKEN&access_token=ACCESS_TOKEN&expires_in=3600&token_type=Bearer
```

Use the `ACCESS_TOKEN` value in the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header for API requests.

# Sample cURL commands

**List a user's tenants:**

```bash
curl -X GET "http://localhost:3000/tenants" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json"
```

**List a vendor's tenants** (as a user belonging to that vendor):

```bash
curl -X GET "http://localhost:3000/tenants?vendor_id=123" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json"
```

payload.output: {"answers":{"flow_scope":{"answers":["What are the tradeoffs of having the auth app set the cookies vs setting the cookies in the org-next app?"]},"cookie_contract":{"answers":["Keep existing names (Recommended)"]},"cookie_domain":{"answers":["Shared parent domains (Recommended)"]}}}

payload.message:
Tradeoff summary:

**Auth app sets cookies**
- Best when auth is meant to be the central session authority for all Anedot browser apps.
- Org-next stays simple: it reads httpOnly sessions and sends `Authorization: Bearer <access token>` to the Rails API.
- Token exchange, refresh, logout, and cross-app SSO can live in one place.
- The cost is coupling: auth must know org-next’s cookie names, TanStack session format, cookie domain, max ages, and the shared session secret used by org-next. A session change in org-next becomes an auth-app contract change.
- It also has a bigger security blast radius: the auth app/worker needs the secret that creates org-next-readable cookies, and shared parent-domain cookies are available to every sibling subdomain unless we namespace them carefully.
- It requires HTTPS subdomains locally. Raw `localhost:3208` → `localhost:3210` will not exercise the same cookie behavior.

**Org-next sets cookies**
- This matches the current auth app spec: auth validates/orchestrates login, returns an authorization code, and the client app exchanges the code for ID/access/refresh tokens.
- The token cookies can be host-only to org-next, so auth never needs org-next’s session secret and cannot accidentally mint app sessions.
- The local setup is easier because org-next controls its callback and cookies.
- The cost is repeated relying-party logic: org-next needs Cognito token endpoint config, state/PKCE handling, refresh logic, and logout cookie clearing. Other client apps would need the same unless we extract a shared package.

My read: if this auth app is intended to become the durable session layer for multiple Anedot apps, auth-set cookies is coherent. If the immediate goal is only “org-next should be authenticated after returning from auth,” org-next-set cookies is the cleaner and less coupled implementation.

content.text:
Tradeoff summary:

**Auth app sets cookies**
- Best when auth is meant to be the central session authority for all Anedot browser apps.
- Org-next stays simple: it reads httpOnly sessions and sends `Authorization: Bearer <access token>` to the Rails API.
- Token exchange, refresh, logout, and cross-app SSO can live in one place.
- The cost is coupling: auth must know org-next’s cookie names, TanStack session format, cookie domain, max ages, and the shared session secret used by org-next. A session change in org-next becomes an auth-app contract change.
- It also has a bigger security blast radius: the auth app/worker needs the secret that creates org-next-readable cookies, and shared parent-domain cookies are available to every sibling subdomain unless we namespace them carefully.
- It requires HTTPS subdomains locally. Raw `localhost:3208` → `localhost:3210` will not exercise the same cookie behavior.

**Org-next sets cookies**
- This matches the current auth app spec: auth validates/orchestrates login, returns an authorization code, and the client app exchanges the code for ID/access/refresh tokens.
- The token cookies can be host-only to org-next, so auth never needs org-next’s session secret and cannot accidentally mint app sessions.
- The local setup is easier because org-next controls its callback and cookies.
- The cost is repeated relying-party logic: org-next needs Cognito token endpoint config, state/PKCE handling, refresh logic, and logout cookie clearing. Other client apps would need the same unless we extract a shared package.

My read: if this auth app is intended to become the durable session layer for multiple Anedot apps, auth-set cookies is coherent. If the immediate goal is only “org-next should be authenticated after returning from auth,” org-next-set cookies is the cleaner and less coupled implementation.

item.text:
# Complete Org-Next/Auth Connection

## Summary
Implement auth-owned session cookies. The auth app will authenticate with real Cognito, mint shared httpOnly token cookies readable by org-next, and redirect users back to their original org-next route. Org-next will only initiate/verify the redirect flow and read cookies. The Rails backend will validate Cognito access tokens.

## Key Changes
- Auth app:
  - Add shared token cookie sessions: existing `authToken` for access token, existing `refreshToken`, and new `idToken`.
  - Add `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET`, separate from `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`, matching org-next `SESSION_SECRET`.
  - Support optional cookie domain: unset for `localhost`, `.anedot.me` locally with subdomains, `.anedot.com` in deployed envs.
  - Allow `http://localhost:<port>` redirect/logout URLs only in local/test; require HTTPS otherwise.
  - Add `return_to` support: validate allowlisted origin plus relative return path, then redirect with `auth_state`.
  - On `/authorize`, refresh from `refreshToken` if access is expired/missing; otherwise show sign-in.
  - On password/MFA/new-password success, set token cookies from Cognito `AuthenticationResult`.
  - For Google/federated, add an auth-owned Cognito callback, exchange the code, set cookies, then return to org-next.
  - On sign-up confirmation, do not store passwords for auto-login; redirect user to sign in.
  - On logout, clear `authToken`, `refreshToken`, `idToken`, auth transaction cookies, then return to org-next `/login?logged_out=1`.

- Org-next:
  - Add `VITE_AUTH_CLIENT_ID`.
  - Change login initiation to create a server-side auth transaction with `state` and `return_to`, then redirect to auth `/authorize`.
  - Preserve protected-route return paths when redirecting to `/login`.
  - Add global return-state verification: consume `auth_state`, verify it against org-next session state, clear it, and continue to the original route without the query param.
  - Add `useIdTokenSession`; keep `authToken` as the access token cookie name for compatibility.
  - Gate `MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN` fallback behind `VITE_TEMP_BYPASS_AUTH`; real mode should require cookies.
  - Change logout from POST-fetching auth `/logout` to browser redirect with `client_id`, allowlisted origin, and `/login?logged_out=1`.

- Backend `/Users/duhl/git/anedot_next`:
  - Replace the current `User.last` auth stub with real Cognito JWT validation.
  - Require `token_use=access`, valid signature, issuer, expiry, and configured client ID.
  - Require an email claim in the access token; return 401 if missing.
  - Find/create users by `cognito_sub`, using the token email for new users.

## Manual Local Tasks
- Configure Cognito app client for real local testing:
  - OAuth code flow enabled for Google/federated callback.
  - Callback URL: `http://localhost:3208/cognito/callback`.
  - Logout URL: `http://localhost:3210/login?logged_out=1`.
  - Scopes include `openid email profile`; access token must include `email`.
- Set auth `.env.local`:
  - `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=false`
  - `COGNITO_CLIENT_ID`, `COGNITO_CLIENT_SECRET`, `COGNITO_DOMAIN`, `COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID`, `COGNITO_REGION`
  - `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET=<same value as org-next SESSION_SECRET>`
  - `AUTH_ALLOWED_URIS_BY_CLIENT_ID={"<client-id>":["http://localhost:3210"]}`
- Set org-next `.env.local`:
  - `VITE_TEMP_BYPASS_AUTH=false`
  - `VITE_AUTH_APP_URL=http://localhost:3208`
  - `VITE_AUTH_CLIENT_ID=<same Cognito client id>`
- Run locally with real Cognito: auth on `localhost:3208`, org-next on `localhost:3210`, backend on `localhost:3000`.

## Test Plan
- Auth unit tests mock Cognito SDK/token responses; CI does not need real Cognito.
- Cover: password success sets all cookies, MFA challenge completion sets cookies, refresh-token path renews cookies, logout clears cookies, localhost exception is local/test only, invalid `return_to` is rejected.
- Org-next tests cover login URL construction, auth-state verification, state mismatch redirect, original-route restoration, id token session reader, and no mock token fallback when bypass is false.
- Backend tests cover missing bearer token, invalid JWT, wrong token type, missing email claim, existing user lookup, and new user creation.
- Manual acceptance: sign in through auth, land on original org-next route, see org API calls authorized by the access token, refresh after access expiry, and logout clears cookies.

## Assumptions And References
- Auth is the session-cookie owner; org-next reads token cookies but does not refresh/write them.
- First implementation uses one Cognito client ID for org-next.
- Existing `authToken` cookie name remains the access token cookie.
- AWS references: [Cognito token endpoint](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/en_us/cognito/latest/developerguide/token-endpoint.html), [localhost callback exception](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/user-pool-settings-client-apps.html), [Cognito access tokens](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/amazon-cognito-user-pools-using-the-access-token.html).

settings.developer_instructions:
# Plan Mode (Conversational)

You work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.

## Mode rules (strict)

You are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.

Plan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.

## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool

Plan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.

Separately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.

## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode

You may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.

### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)

Actions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs
* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration
* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files
* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files

### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)

Actions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Editing or writing files
* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files
* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files
* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it

When in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as "doing the work" rather than "planning the work," do not do it.

## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)

Begin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.

Before asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.

Exception: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.

Do not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, "where is this struct?" or "which UI component should we use?" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.

## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)

* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.
* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.

## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)

* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.

## Asking questions

Critical rules:

* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.
* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.
* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.

You SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:

* materially change the spec/plan, OR
* confirm/lock an assumption, OR
* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.
* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.

Use the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.

## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)

1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.

   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.
   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.
   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).

2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.

   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.
   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.
   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.

## Finalization rule

Only output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.

When you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:

1) The opening tag must be on its own line.
2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).
3) The closing tag must be on its own line.
4) Use Markdown inside the block.
5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.

Example:

<proposed_plan>
plan content
</proposed_plan>

plan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:

* A clear title
* A brief summary section
* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types
* Test cases and scenarios
* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed

When possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.

Prefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.

Keep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.

Do not ask "should I proceed?" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.

Only produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.

If the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.

This section maps Claude Code plugin tool references to Codex behavior.

- Read: use shell reads (cat/sed) or rg
- Write: create files via shell redirection or apply_patch
- Edit/MultiEdit: use apply_patch
- Bash: use shell_command
- Grep: use rg (fallback: grep)

- LS: use ls via shell_command
- WebFetch/WebSearch: use curl or Context7 for library docs
- AskUserQuestion/Question: ask the user in chat
- Task/Subagent/Parallel: run sequentially in main thread; use multi_tool_use.parallel for tool calls
- TodoWrite/TodoRead: use file-based todos in todos/ with file-todos skill
- Skill: open the referenced SKILL.md and follow it
- ExitPlanMode: ignore
<!-- END COMPOUND CODEX TOOL MAP -->

--- project-doc ---

## Style and typing

- For React forms, never use `FormEvent`. Use `SubmitEvent<HTMLFormElement>` type instead.
- Always use string literals over string concatenation.

## Docs-first context

- Before making non-trivial changes in `apps/org-next`, read `apps/org-next/docs/README.md` and the most relevant files under `apps/org-next/docs/` to gain feature and architecture context.
- Treat `apps/org-next/docs/` as a maintained context layer that complements the code. Use it to understand routes, flows, server dependencies, and assumptions before editing.

<app-context>
# Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.

### Review findings
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive to emit inline code review findings (or when a user asks you to call out specific lines).
- Emit one directive per finding; emit none when there are no findings.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3), confidence (0-1).
- priority/confidence are for review findings; omit when you're just pointing at a location without a finding.
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2 confidence=0.55}

### Archiving
- If a user specifically asks you to end a thread/conversation, you can return the archive directive ::archive{...} to archive the thread/conversation.
- Example: ::archive{reason="User requested to end conversation"}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.
</app-context>

item.text:
# Complete Org-Next/Auth Connection

## Summary
Implement auth-owned session cookies for the non-federated Cognito username/password flow. The auth app will authenticate with Cognito, mint shared httpOnly token cookies readable by org-next, and redirect users back to their original org-next route. Google/federated auth is explicitly out of scope for this pass.

## Key Changes
- Auth app:
  - Add shared token cookie sessions: existing `authToken` for access token, existing `refreshToken`, and new `idToken`.
  - Add `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET`, separate from `AUTH_SESSION_SECRET`, matching org-next `SESSION_SECRET`.
  - Support optional cookie domain: unset for `localhost`, `.anedot.me` locally with subdomains, `.anedot.com` in deployed envs.
  - Allow `http://localhost:<port>` redirect/logout URLs only in local/test; require HTTPS otherwise.
  - Add `return_to` support: validate allowlisted origin plus relative return path, then redirect with `auth_state`.
  - On password, MFA, or new-password success, set token cookies from Cognito `AuthenticationResult`.
  - On `/authorize`, if refresh token cookie exists and access is missing/expired, refresh through Cognito and return to org-next; otherwise show sign-in.
  - On sign-up confirmation, do not store passwords for auto-login; redirect user to sign in.
  - On logout, clear `authToken`, `refreshToken`, `idToken`, and auth transaction cookies, then return to org-next `/login?logged_out=1`.
  - Remove/defer Google/federated callback, hosted UI redirect, and `identity_provider` work from this implementation.

- Org-next:
  - Add `VITE_AUTH_CLIENT_ID`.
  - Change login initiation to create a server-side auth transaction with `state` and `return_to`, then redirect to auth `/authorize`.
  - Preserve protected-route return paths when redirecting to `/login`.
  - Add global return-state verification: consume `auth_state`, verify it against org-next session state, clear it, and continue to the original route without the query param.
  - Add `useIdTokenSession`; keep `authToken` as the access token cookie name for compatibility.
  - Gate `MOCK_ACCESS_TOKEN` fallback behind `VITE_TEMP_BYPASS_AUTH`; real mode should require cookies.
  - Change logout from POST-fetching auth `/logout` to browser redirect with `client_id`, allowlisted origin, and `/login?logged_out=1`.

- Backend `/Users/duhl/git/anedot_next`:
  - Replace the current `User.last` auth stub with real Cognito JWT validation.
  - Require `token_use=access`, valid signature, issuer, expiry, and configured client ID.
  - Require an email claim in the access token; return 401 if missing.
  - Find/create users by `cognito_sub`, using the token email for new users.

## Manual Local Tasks
- Configure Cognito for username/password SRP auth:
  - App client allows the auth flow used by `USER_SRP_AUTH`.
  - Access token includes `email`.
  - No Google/federated callback setup is required for this pass.
- Set auth `.env.local`:
  - `VITE_AUTH_MOCK_MODE=false`
  - `COGNITO_CLIENT_ID`, `COGNITO_CLIENT_SECRET`, `COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID`, `COGNITO_REGION`
  - `ORG_NEXT_SESSION_SECRET=<same value as org-next SESSION_SECRET>`
  - `AUTH_ALLOWED_URIS_BY_CLIENT_ID={"<client-id>":["http://localhost:3210"]}`
- Set org-next `.env.local`:
  - `VITE_TEMP_BYPASS_AUTH=false`
  - `VITE_AUTH_APP_URL=http://localhost:3208`
  - `VITE_AUTH_CLIENT_ID=<same Cognito client id>`
- Run locally with real Cognito: auth on `localhost:3208`, org-next on `localhost:3210`, backend on `localhost:3000`.

## Test Plan
- Auth tests mock Cognito SDK responses; CI does not need real Cognito.
- Cover: password success sets all cookies, MFA challenge completion sets cookies, new-password completion sets cookies, refresh renews cookies, logout clears cookies, localhost exception is local/test only, invalid `return_to` is rejected.
- Org-next tests cover login URL construction, auth-state verification, state mismatch redirect, original-route restoration, id token session reader, and no mock token fallback when bypass is false.
- Backend tests cover missing bearer token, invalid JWT, wrong token type, missing email claim, existing user lookup, and new user creation.
- Manual acceptance: sign in through auth, land on original org-next route, see org API calls authorized by the access token, refresh after access expiry, and logout clears cookies.

## Assumptions
- Auth is the session-cookie owner; org-next reads token cookies but does not refresh/write them.
- Existing `authToken` cookie name remains the access token cookie.
- Google/federated auth, Cognito Hosted UI redirects, and hosted callback handling are deferred.
- Docs in `apps/org-next/docs/` and `apps/auth/docs/` must be updated to match this narrowed contract.

settings.developer_instructions:
# Plan Mode (Conversational)

You work in 3 phases, and you should *chat your way* to a great plan before finalizing it. A great plan is very detailed—intent- and implementation-wise—so that it can be handed to another engineer or agent to be implemented right away. It must be **decision complete**, where the implementer does not need to make any decisions.

## Mode rules (strict)

You are in **Plan Mode** until a developer message explicitly ends it.

Plan Mode is not changed by user intent, tone, or imperative language. If a user asks for execution while still in Plan Mode, treat it as a request to **plan the execution**, not perform it.

## Plan Mode vs update_plan tool

Plan Mode is a collaboration mode that can involve requesting user input and eventually issuing a `<proposed_plan>` block.

Separately, `update_plan` is a checklist/progress/TODOs tool; it does not enter or exit Plan Mode. Do not confuse it with Plan mode or try to use it while in Plan mode. If you try to use `update_plan` in Plan mode, it will return an error.

## Execution vs. mutation in Plan Mode

You may explore and execute **non-mutating** actions that improve the plan. You must not perform **mutating** actions.

### Allowed (non-mutating, plan-improving)

Actions that gather truth, reduce ambiguity, or validate feasibility without changing repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Reading or searching files, configs, schemas, types, manifests, and docs
* Static analysis, inspection, and repo exploration
* Dry-run style commands when they do not edit repo-tracked files
* Tests, builds, or checks that may write to caches or build artifacts (for example, `target/`, `.cache/`, or snapshots) so long as they do not edit repo-tracked files

### Not allowed (mutating, plan-executing)

Actions that implement the plan or change repo-tracked state. Examples:

* Editing or writing files
* Running formatters or linters that rewrite files
* Applying patches, migrations, or codegen that updates repo-tracked files
* Side-effectful commands whose purpose is to carry out the plan rather than refine it

When in doubt: if the action would reasonably be described as "doing the work" rather than "planning the work," do not do it.

## PHASE 1 — Ground in the environment (explore first, ask second)

Begin by grounding yourself in the actual environment. Eliminate unknowns in the prompt by discovering facts, not by asking the user. Resolve all questions that can be answered through exploration or inspection. Identify missing or ambiguous details only if they cannot be derived from the environment. Silent exploration between turns is allowed and encouraged.

Before asking the user any question, perform at least one targeted non-mutating exploration pass (for example: search relevant files, inspect likely entrypoints/configs, confirm current implementation shape), unless no local environment/repo is available.

Exception: you may ask clarifying questions about the user's prompt before exploring, ONLY if there are obvious ambiguities or contradictions in the prompt itself. However, if ambiguity might be resolved by exploring, always prefer exploring first.

Do not ask questions that can be answered from the repo or system (for example, "where is this struct?" or "which UI component should we use?" when exploration can make it clear). Only ask once you have exhausted reasonable non-mutating exploration.

## PHASE 2 — Intent chat (what they actually want)

* Keep asking until you can clearly state: goal + success criteria, audience, in/out of scope, constraints, current state, and the key preferences/tradeoffs.
* Bias toward questions over guessing: if any high-impact ambiguity remains, do NOT plan yet—ask.

## PHASE 3 — Implementation chat (what/how we’ll build)

* Once intent is stable, keep asking until the spec is decision complete: approach, interfaces (APIs/schemas/I/O), data flow, edge cases/failure modes, testing + acceptance criteria, rollout/monitoring, and any migrations/compat constraints.

## Asking questions

Critical rules:

* Strongly prefer using the `request_user_input` tool to ask any questions.
* Offer only meaningful multiple‑choice options; don’t include filler choices that are obviously wrong or irrelevant.
* In rare cases where an unavoidable, important question can’t be expressed with reasonable multiple‑choice options (due to extreme ambiguity), you may ask it directly without the tool.

You SHOULD ask many questions, but each question must:

* materially change the spec/plan, OR
* confirm/lock an assumption, OR
* choose between meaningful tradeoffs.
* not be answerable by non-mutating commands.

Use the `request_user_input` tool only for decisions that materially change the plan, for confirming important assumptions, or for information that cannot be discovered via non-mutating exploration.

## Two kinds of unknowns (treat differently)

1. **Discoverable facts** (repo/system truth): explore first.

   * Before asking, run targeted searches and check likely sources of truth (configs/manifests/entrypoints/schemas/types/constants).
   * Ask only if: multiple plausible candidates; nothing found but you need a missing identifier/context; or ambiguity is actually product intent.
   * If asking, present concrete candidates (paths/service names) + recommend one.
   * Never ask questions you can answer from your environment (e.g., “where is this struct”).

2. **Preferences/tradeoffs** (not discoverable): ask early.

   * These are intent or implementation preferences that cannot be derived from exploration.
   * Provide 2–4 mutually exclusive options + a recommended default.
   * If unanswered, proceed with the recommended option and record it as an assumption in the final plan.

## Finalization rule

Only output the final plan when it is decision complete and leaves no decisions to the implementer.

When you present the official plan, wrap it in a `<proposed_plan>` block so the client can render it specially:

1) The opening tag must be on its own line.
2) Start the plan content on the next line (no text on the same line as the tag).
3) The closing tag must be on its own line.
4) Use Markdown inside the block.
5) Keep the tags exactly as `<proposed_plan>` and `</proposed_plan>` (do not translate or rename them), even if the plan content is in another language.

Example:

<proposed_plan>
plan content
</proposed_plan>

plan content should be human and agent digestible. The final plan must be plan-only, concise by default, and include:

* A clear title
* A brief summary section
* Important changes or additions to public APIs/interfaces/types
* Test cases and scenarios
* Explicit assumptions and defaults chosen where needed

When possible, prefer a compact structure with 3-5 short sections, usually: Summary, Key Changes or Implementation Changes, Test Plan, and Assumptions. Do not include a separate Scope section unless scope boundaries are genuinely important to avoid mistakes.

Prefer grouped implementation bullets by subsystem or behavior over file-by-file inventories. Mention files only when needed to disambiguate a non-obvious change, and avoid naming more than 3 paths unless extra specificity is necessary to prevent mistakes. Prefer behavior-level descriptions over symbol-by-symbol removal lists. For v1 feature-addition plans, do not invent detailed schema, validation, precedence, fallback, or wire-shape policy unless the request establishes it or it is needed to prevent a concrete implementation mistake; prefer the intended capability and minimum interface/behavior changes.

Keep bullets short and avoid explanatory sub-bullets unless they are needed to prevent ambiguity. Prefer the minimum detail needed for implementation safety, not exhaustive coverage. Within each section, compress related changes into a few high-signal bullets and omit branch-by-branch logic, repeated invariants, and long lists of unaffected behavior unless they are necessary to prevent a likely implementation mistake. Avoid repeated repo facts and irrelevant edge-case or rollout detail. For straightforward refactors, keep the plan to a compact summary, key edits, tests, and assumptions. If the user asks for more detail, then expand.

Do not ask "should I proceed?" in the final output. The user can easily switch out of Plan mode and request implementation if you have included a `<proposed_plan>` block in your response. Alternatively, they can decide to stay in Plan mode and continue refining the plan.

Only produce at most one `<proposed_plan>` block per turn, and only when you are presenting a complete spec.

If the user stays in Plan mode and asks for revisions after a prior `<proposed_plan>`, any new `<proposed_plan>` must be a complete replacement.

payload.message:
# In app browser:
- The user has the in-app browser open.
- Current URL: not currently navigated.

## My request for Codex:
I don't understand this point: On sign-up confirmation, do not store passwords for auto-login; redirect user to sign in.

That point is about what happens after a new user creates an account and enters the confirmation code.

To set the access/refresh/id token cookies immediately after confirmation, auth would need to authenticate the user. Cognito confirmation alone does not return tokens. One way to auto-login would be to keep the user’s plaintext password from the sign-up form in the auth transaction session until confirmation succeeds, then use it to sign in. I’m recommending we do **not** do that, because storing a password, even briefly in an encrypted httpOnly session cookie, is unnecessary risk.

So the planned behavior is:

1. User signs up.
2. Cognito sends confirmation code.
3. User enters code.
4. Auth confirms the account.
5. Auth sends the user back to the sign-in screen.
6. User enters credentials once to create the token cookies.

We can phrase it more clearly in the plan as:

“After real sign-up confirmation, show a success notice and redirect to sign-in. Do not auto-login confirmed users in this pass, because Cognito confirmation does not issue tokens and auto-login would require temporarily retaining the sign-up password.”
